trajectory_description: The research tree traces two converging lines: the development of vision-language models (from Qwen-VL and Flamingo to Qwen3-VL) and video understanding systems (VTimeLLM, TimeChat, LLaMA-VID, leading to TimeMarker), alongside supporting efforts in instruction tuning, temporal grounding, OCR benchmarks, and self-supervised learning. These strands culminate in the frontier paper, Cosmos 3 (2026), which integrates multimodal perception, temporal reasoning, and world modeling into an "omnimodal" architecture for physical AI. The overall narrative reflects a progression from isolated multimodal tasks and benchmarks toward unified, temporally-aware world models capable of grounding language, vision, and video in real-world interaction.

4a5ca9c50aa13bc4b0014c775197ed5079fec167.title: Cosmos 3: Omnimodal World Models for Physical AI

entities.name: Cosmos 3
entities.description: A family of omnimodal world models that jointly process and generate language, image, video, audio, and action sequences using a mixture-of-transformers architecture.

entities.name: Physical AI
entities.description: Domain of embodied agents requiring understanding and generation across multiple modalities such as vision, language, and action.

entities.name: Mixture-of-Transformers
entities.description: Unified architecture that subsumes vision-language models, video generators, world simulators, and world-action models.

idea.background: Previous work in Physical AI has relied on separate models for vision-language understanding, video generation, world simulation, and action planning, lacking a unified framework. Cosmos 3 addresses this by integrating all these capabilities into a single omnimodal world model.
idea.novelty: Cosmos 3 introduces a family of omnimodal world models that jointly handle language, image, video, audio, and action sequences within a unified mixture-of-transformers architecture, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple tasks. This is the first work to subsume vision-language models, video generators, world simulators, and world-action models into one framework.
idea.contribution: The primary contribution is the development of Cosmos 3, a family of omnimodal world models built on a mixture-of-transformers architecture. It seamlessly integrates understanding and generation tasks for language, image, video, audio, and action, providing a scalable, general-purpose backbone for embodied agents.
idea.method: Cosmos 3 employs a mixture-of-transformers architecture that processes multiple modalities in a unified manner. The model is trained on large-scale multimodal data, enabling flexible input-output configurations. Key design choices include joint representation learning across modalities and post-training for specific tasks, achieving best open-source Text-to-Image and Image-to-Video performance.
idea.detail_reason: The mixture-of-transformers architecture allows efficient handling of diverse modalities with shared parameters, enabling cross-modal learning and task transfer. Flexible input-output configurations make the model adaptable to various Physical AI tasks without architectural changes, improving scalability and generalization.
idea.limitation: The abstract does not discuss specific limitations, but potential shortcomings include reliance on large-scale curated synthetic datasets and the computational cost of training such a unified model. Additionally, evaluation may not cover all real-world scenarios for embodied agents.

experiment.process: The model was evaluated on a diverse suite of understanding and generation tasks, including text-to-image, image-to-video, and policy learning for embodied agents. Post-trained models were ranked by Artificial Analysis and RoboArena.
experiment.technical_details: The architecture uses mixture-of-transformers, trained on curated synthetic datasets and benchmarked against state-of-the-art models in text-to-image, image-to-video, and policy domains.
experiment.baseline: Comparisons are made with existing vision-language models, video generators, world simulators, and world-action models, though specific baselines are not listed in the abstract.
experiment.verification: State-of-the-art results across multiple tasks demonstrate the effectiveness of the unified framework. Rankings by independent evaluators (Artificial Analysis, RoboArena) provide external verification.

references: N/A

entities.description: Vision-language model family with dense (2B/4B/8B/32B) and MoE (30B-A3B/235B-A22B) variants, supporting 256K token interleaved contexts.

entities.name: Interleaved-MRoPE
entities.description: Enhanced rotary position embedding for stronger spatial-temporal modeling across images and video.

entities.name: DeepStack
entities.description: Integration technique for improved model architecture and performance.

idea.background: Vision-language models have advanced significantly, with models like Qwen2-VL and LLaVA showing strong multimodal understanding. The Qwen series aims to push performance further, especially in long-context and complex reasoning tasks.
idea.novelty: Qwen3-VL introduces three core pillars: superior pure-text understanding, robust 256K-token long-context comprehension for interleaved multimodal inputs, and advanced multimodal reasoning. Architecturally, it features enhanced interleaved-MRoPE and DeepStack integration, not present in prior Qwen models.
idea.contribution: The primary methods include an enhanced interleaved-MRoPE for spatial-temporal modeling, DeepStack integration for architectural improvements, and a mixture-of-experts variant for flexible latency-quality trade-offs. These components collectively enable state-of-the-art performance on comprehensive benchmarks.
idea.method: The architecture includes both dense and mixture-of-experts variants. It natively supports interleaved contexts of up to 256K tokens using interleaved-MRoPE for positional encoding. The training procedure is not detailed, but the model achieves strong results on MMMU, MathVista, and MathVision benchmarks.
idea.detail_reason: The interleaved-MRoPE enhances the model's ability to capture spatial and temporal relationships across images and video, while DeepStack improves overall model capacity. The MoE variants allow efficient scaling without proportional compute cost, enabling deployment across latency-quality scenarios.
idea.limitation: The abstract does not discuss specific shortcomings. Potential limitations include inference overhead for MoE variants and possible performance degradation on tasks requiring extremely long-range dependencies beyond 256K tokens. The model's behavior on low-resource languages or niche domains is not addressed.

experiment.process: The model was evaluated on a broad range of multimodal benchmarks including MMMU, MathVista, and MathVision.
experiment.technical_details: Not specified in the abstract beyond the model architecture and context length.
experiment.baseline: Comparison likely includes prior Qwen models and other state-of-the-art vision-language models.
experiment.verification: Superior performance on comprehensive benchmarks demonstrates the effectiveness of the architectural innovations.

ee57e4d7a125f4ca8916284a857c3760d7d378d3.title: Self-Supervised Learning from Images with a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture

entities.name: I-JEPA
entities.description: Image-based Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for self-supervised learning from images.

entities.name: Vision Transformer (ViT)
entities.description: Transformer architecture for image processing, used as backbone.

entities.name: context block
entities.description: Spatially distributed region in an image used as input to predict representations of target blocks.

entities.name: target block
entities.description: Large-scale semantic region in an image whose representation is predicted from the context block.

idea.background: Self-supervised learning from images typically relies on hand-crafted data augmentations to learn semantic representations. Previous work includes generative and contrastive approaches, often requiring extensive augmentation strategies.
idea.novelty: I-JEPA introduces a non-generative approach that predicts representations of target blocks from a single context block without using hand-crafted data augmentations. The key innovation is the masking strategy that samples large-scale semantic target blocks and uses a sufficiently informative spatially distributed context block.
idea.contribution: The primary method is I-JEPA, which learns image representations by predicting the representations of multiple target blocks from one context block within the same image. Core components include the masking strategy to select target and context blocks, and the use of Vision Transformers as the backbone architecture.
idea.method: I-JEPA uses a Vision Transformer (ViT) as the encoder. During training, a single context block (a spatially distributed region) is encoded, and its output representations are used to predict the representations of several target blocks (large-scale regions) via a predictor module. The masking strategy is crucial: target blocks are sampled at sufficiently large scale to capture semantic content, and the context block is chosen to be informative (spatially distributed). The model is trained with a simple mean squared error loss between predicted and actual target representations.
idea.detail_reason: Large target blocks ensure that the predicted representations capture high-level semantic information rather than low-level details. The spatially distributed context block provides diverse contextual cues, enabling the model to infer missing semantic content. This design avoids reliance on hand-crafted augmentations and scales well with large Vision Transformers.
idea.limitation: The method requires careful tuning of the masking strategy (e.g., block size and location) to balance semantics and difficulty. It may struggle with images that have multiple disconnected semantic regions or require fine-grained discrimination. Additionally, the approach is evaluated only on ImageNet and a few downstream tasks; generalization to other domains is not fully explored.

experiment.process: I-JEPA was trained on ImageNet using a Vision Transformer backbone. The model was evaluated on downstream tasks including linear classification, object counting, and depth prediction.
experiment.technical_details: A ViT-Huge/14 model was trained on 16 A100 GPUs for under 72 hours. The masking strategy used large target blocks (e.g., covering a significant portion of the image) and a spatially distributed context block.
experiment.baseline: Comparisons were made against other self-supervised methods (e.g., SimCLR, MAE, DINO) on linear classification and other tasks, though specific baselines are not listed in the abstract.
experiment.verification: The strong performance across diverse tasks (linear classification, object counting, depth prediction) demonstrates that I-JEPA learns highly semantic representations that transfer well.

references: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale
Masked Autoencoders Are Scalable Vision Learners
Bootstrap Your Own Latent: A New Approach to Self-Supervised Learning

fc6a2f7478f68adefd69e2071f27e38aa1647f2f.title: Qwen-VL: A Versatile Vision-Language Model for Understanding, Localization, Text Reading, and Beyond

entities.name: Qwen-VL
entities.description: Large-scale vision-language model for understanding images and text

entities.name: Qwen-VL-Chat
entities.description: Instruction-tuned version of Qwen-VL for conversational tasks

entities.name: Qwen-LM
entities.description: Large language model foundation used for Qwen-VL

entities.name: Visual receptor
entities.description: Component for visual perception

entities.name: Input-output interface
entities.description: Interface for multimodal interaction

entities.name: 3-stage training pipeline
entities.description: Three-stage training process for vision-language alignment

entities.name: Multilingual multimodal cleaned corpus
entities.description: Cleaned and diverse dataset for training

idea.background: Large-scale vision-language models (LVLMs) aim to perceive and understand both texts and images. Previous models like BLIP-2 and LLaVA have shown success, but often lack fine-grained grounding and text-reading abilities. This work builds on the Qwen-LM language model.
idea.novelty: Qwen-VL introduces grounding and text-reading capability by aligning image-caption-box tuples, enabling precise localization and optical character recognition. It also employs a meticulously designed 3-stage training pipeline and a multilingual multimodal cleaned corpus, achieving new state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks while maintaining generalist performance.
idea.contribution: The primary methods include a visual receptor for image encoding, an input-output interface for interacting with the language model, and a 3-stage training pipeline that progressively aligns vision and language. Additionally, a large-scale multilingual multimodal cleaned corpus is used for data diversity. These components enable strong performance on image captioning, question answering, visual grounding, and text reading.
idea.method: The architecture starts from the Qwen-LM as a foundation, augmented with a visual receptor (likely a vision transformer) and a cross-modal projection. Training follows a 3-stage pipeline: (1) vision-language pretraining on image-caption pairs, (2) multi-task training with grounding data (image-caption-box tuples), and (3) instruction tuning for conversational ability. Key design choices include using multilingual data and a cleaned corpus to reduce noise.
idea.detail_reason: The 3-stage training allows the model to first learn general vision-language alignment, then specialize in grounding and text reading through box-supervised learning, and finally adapt to dialog. The use of aligned image-caption-box tuples is effective because it provides explicit spatial supervision, enabling the model to localize objects and read text accurately, as evidenced by superior benchmark results.
idea.limitation: The abstract does not discuss limitations explicitly, but potential shortcomings include dependency on large-scale annotated data (box annotations) and computational cost for training. The model's performance on extremely fine-grained or domain-specific tasks may be limited, and generalization to unseen languages or scenarios remains to be explored.

experiment.process: Evaluation was conducted on a broad range of visual-centric benchmarks including image captioning (e.g., COCO), question answering (e.g., VQA), visual grounding (e.g., RefCOCO), and text reading tasks. Both zero-shot and few-shot settings were tested. Additionally, real-world dialog benchmarks were used to assess the instruction-tuned Qwen-VL-Chat.
experiment.technical_details: Model scales are comparable to existing generalist LVLMs (e.g., around 7B parameters). Specific technologies include the Qwen-LM backbone, a visual encoder (likely ViT), and a cross-attention or Q-Former mechanism for modality fusion. Training data includes public datasets as well as the proposed cleaned multilingual corpus.
experiment.baseline: The models were compared against generalist models of similar scale, such as BLIP-2, LLaVA, and other LVLMs. Qwen-VL and Qwen-VL-Chat set new records across multiple benchmarks under zero-shot and few-shot settings.
experiment.verification: The core idea of aligning image-caption-box tuples and the 3-stage pipeline is verified by achieving state-of-the-art performance on grounding and text reading tasks, while maintaining strong results on standard vision-language tasks, demonstrating that the design successfully integrates these capabilities without sacrificing generality.

references: BLIP-2: Bootstrapping Language-Image Pre-training with Frozen Image Encoders and Large Language Models
LLaVA: Large Language and Vision Assistant
Flamingo: a Visual Language Model for Few-Shot Learning

entities.name: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs)
entities.description: Models evaluated on OCR capabilities, e.g., text localization and reasoning.

idea.background: Existing benchmarks like OCRBench evaluate LMMs on text recognition but underexplore challenging tasks such as text localization, handwritten content extraction, and logical reasoning. OCRBench v2 addresses these gaps with more comprehensive tasks and scenarios.
idea.novelty: OCRBench v2 includes 4x more tasks than OCRBench, covers 31 diverse scenarios, provides bilingual data, high proportion of difficult samples, and a private test set for reliability validation.
idea.contribution: Introduces a large-scale benchmark with 10,000 human-verified QA pairs and 1,500 private images. Evaluates state-of-the-art LMMs, revealing that most score below 50 out of 100 and suffer from five types of limitations.
idea.method: The abstract does not detail the method. The benchmark is constructed by human annotation and verification, with both public and private test sets to ensure consistent evaluation trends.
idea.detail_reason: Not available from abstract. The benchmark design ensures reliability via consistent trends across public and private sets, indicating robust evaluation.
idea.limitation: Most LMMs score below 50, indicating poor performance. Five types of limitations are identified, including less frequently encountered text recognition and fine-grained perception (abstract truncated).

experiment.process: State-of-the-art LMMs are benchmarked on both public and private test sets of OCRBench v2, and evaluation trends are compared for consistency.
experiment.technical_details: Not specified in abstract.
experiment.baseline: State-of-the-art Large Multimodal Models (LMMs).
experiment.verification: Consistent evaluation trends across public and private test sets validate the benchmark's reliability.

references: OCRBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Large Multimodal Models on Optical Character Recognition
TextVQA: A Dataset for Reasoning about Text in Visual Questions
Scene Text Recognition: A Survey

a59e54274900ec3822a425a5e52a97efccc0320c.title: TimeMarker: A Versatile Video-LLM for Long and Short Video Understanding with Superior Temporal Localization Ability

entities.name: TimeMarker
entities.description: A versatile Video-LLM for temporal localization in videos of varying lengths.

entities.name: Temporal Separator Tokens
entities.description: Tokens that enhance temporal awareness by marking specific moments in videos.

entities.name: AnyLength mechanism
entities.description: A mechanism for dynamic frame sampling and adaptive token merging to handle short and long videos.

idea.background: Large language models have advanced multimodal LLMs, but existing video-language models often lack precise temporal localization and struggle with videos of different lengths.
idea.novelty: TimeMarker introduces Temporal Separator Tokens for marking moments and AnyLength mechanism for dynamic frame sampling and adaptive token merging, enabling effective handling of both short and long videos.
idea.contribution: TimeMarker is designed for high-quality dialogue with temporal localization. Core components include Temporal Separator Tokens for temporal awareness and AnyLength mechanism for handling variable video lengths.
idea.method: The architecture integrates Temporal Separator Tokens into a Video-LLM to mark specific moments. The AnyLength mechanism dynamically samples frames and merges tokens adaptively. Training uses diverse datasets including transformed temporal-related video QA, image, and interleaved data to enhance temporal and semantic understanding.
idea.detail_reason: Temporal Separator Tokens provide explicit temporal markers, improving temporal localization accuracy. AnyLength mechanism efficiently processes videos of any length by adjusting frame sampling and token merging.
idea.limitation: Potential limitations include reliance on predefined temporal separators and possible performance degradation on extremely long or complex temporal reasoning tasks.

experiment.process: Evaluated on multiple benchmarks covering short and long video categories to assess temporal localization and dialogue quality.

idea.background: Existing benchmarks for evaluating LMMs in OCR are limited by narrow scenarios and specified tasks. There is a lack of a comprehensive benchmark that covers diverse scenarios, tasks, and challenges to effectively measure literate capabilities of LMMs.
idea.novelty: CC-OCR introduces a comprehensive benchmark with 39 subsets and 7,058 fully annotated images, 41% from real applications. It covers four OCR-centric tracks and evaluates 14 prominent LMMs and 2 specialists, revealing strengths and weaknesses in text grounding, multi-orientation, and hallucination of repetition.
idea.contribution: The primary method is the construction of the CC-OCR benchmark, which comprises four tracks: multi-scene text reading, multilingual text reading, document parsing, and key information extraction. 376461a2c049f6fa51a4303853fdc672e4d07a0d.title: Thinking in Space: How Multimodal Large Language Models See, Remember, and Recall Spaces

entities.name: VSI-Bench
entities.description: Video-based visual-spatial intelligence benchmark with over 5,000 question-answer pairs.

entities.name: MLLMs
entities.description: Multimodal Large Language Models trained on million-scale video datasets.

entities.name: Cognitive maps
entities.description: Explicitly generated spatial representations during question-answering to enhance spatial distance ability.

entities.name: Spatial reasoning
entities.description: Primary bottleneck limiting MLLM performance on visual-spatial tasks.

entities.name: World models
entities.description: Local world models that emerge within MLLMs as internal representations of spatial environments.

idea.background: Humans possess visual-spatial intelligence to remember spaces from sequential visual observations. Prior work has explored spatial reasoning in AI, but it remains unclear whether MLLMs trained on video data can develop similar capabilities. Existing benchmarks often focus on static images or simpler spatial tasks.
idea.novelty: This paper introduces VSI-Bench, the first video-based benchmark for evaluating visual-spatial intelligence in MLLMs, finding that while MLLMs show emergent spatial awareness, they still underperform humans. A key innovation is that generating explicit cognitive maps during QA improves spatial distance ability, whereas standard linguistic reasoning techniques (e.g., chain-of-thought) do not help.
idea.contribution: The primary contributions are: (1) a novel benchmark VSI-Bench with over 5,000 QA pairs from videos; (2) a probing methodology to analyze how MLLMs express spatial thinking linguistically and visually; (3) the finding that explicit cognitive map generation enhances spatial performance, while chain-of-thought reasoning fails.
idea.method: The method involves constructing VSI-Bench by curating video sequences and designing questions that test spatial memory and reasoning. Multiple MLLMs are evaluated on the benchmark. The key intervention is to prompt models to generate cognitive maps (e.g., free-form spatial sketches) before answering questions. No specific model architecture is introduced; instead, the focus is on probing and prompting existing MLLMs.
idea.detail_reason: Generating cognitive maps is effective because it forces the model to externalize its internal spatial representation, compensating for the lack of explicit spatial reasoning modules. Linguistic reasoning techniques like chain-of-thought may not align with the visuospatial nature of the task, explaining their ineffectiveness.
idea.limitation: MLLMs still exhibit subhuman performance, indicating a fundamental bottleneck in spatial reasoning capabilities. The study does not address how to improve internal representations or training objectives to enhance world models. The cognitive map prompting may not generalize to all spatial tasks or model architectures.

experiment.process: Experiments evaluate multiple MLLMs on VSI-Bench, comparing performance to human baselines. Different prompting strategies (chain-of-thought, self-consistency, tree-of-thoughts, cognitive map generation) are tested. Models are probed for linguistic and visual expressions of spatial understanding.
experiment.technical_details: VSI-Bench consists of video clips and multiple-choice or open-ended questions. Models are queried with and without explicit instruction to generate cognitive maps. Performance metrics include accuracy and correlation with human judgments.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include human performance, standard QA prompts, and fixed-pipeline LLMs without vision. Linguistic reasoning baselines: chain-of-thought, self-consistency, tree-of-thoughts.
experiment.verification: The core idea—that MLLMs have emergent spatial awareness but limited reasoning—is verified by showing that cognitive map generation improves distance estimation, while linguistic reasoning does not outperform standard prompts. The benchmark covers diverse spatial skills (e.g., distance, orientation, layout).

references: Visual Spatial Intelligence: A New Benchmark for Vision-Language Models
Multimodal Large Language Models: A Survey
World Models: A Review of Predictive Learning in Autonomous Systems

17ff6a0844afe74796022e7aaf372553e9303d72.title: VTimeLLM: Empower LLM to Grasp Video Moments

entities.name: VTimeLLM
entities.description: A Video LLM designed for fine-grained video moment understanding and reasoning with respect to time boundary.

entities.name: Temporal Video Grounding
entities.description: A task that requires identifying the start and end times of specific events in videos.

entities.name: Dense Video Captioning
entities.description: A task that involves generating detailed descriptions for multiple events in a video along with their time boundaries.

idea.background: Large language models (LLMs) have been extended to Video LLMs for video understanding, but existing models can only provide coarse whole-video descriptions and fail to capture precise start and end time boundaries of specific events. This limits their performance in fine-grained temporal comprehension tasks.
idea.novelty: VTimeLLM introduces a boundary-aware three-stage training strategy that explicitly enhances temporal-boundary awareness, enabling the model to understand and reason about fine-grained video moments. This is a significant improvement over prior Video LLMs that lack time boundary sensitivity.
idea.contribution: The primary methods include a three-stage training process: first, image-text pairs for feature alignment; second, multiple-event videos to increase temporal-boundary awareness; third, high-quality video-instruction tuning to improve temporal understanding and align with human intents. These components together enable precise temporal grounding and reasoning.
idea.method: VTimeLLM adopts a three-stage training strategy. Stage 1 uses image-text pairs to align visual and textual features. Stage 2 employs videos with multiple events to train the model to recognize temporal boundaries between events. Stage 3 uses instruction tuning on high-quality video datasets to refine temporal understanding and follow human instructions. The architecture builds upon a standard Video LLM but with modifications to handle time boundary inputs and outputs.
idea.detail_reason: The three-stage training progressively builds temporal awareness: alignment provides basic understanding, boundary-aware training sharpens event segmentation, and instruction tuning ensures the model responds accurately to time-related queries. This gradual approach avoids catastrophic forgetting and improves performance on fine-grained tasks.
idea.limitation: The abstract does not discuss limitations explicitly. Potential limitations may include reliance on high-quality annotated video-instruction data, which is scarce, and possible difficulties in handling very long or complex videos with many overlapping events. The model's performance on out-of-domain videos is also not addressed.

experiment.process: Experiments were conducted on Temporal Video Grounding and Dense Video Captioning benchmarks. The model's performance was compared against existing Video LLMs and task-specific methods.
experiment.technical_details: VTimeLLM was trained using the three-stage strategy with datasets including image-text pairs, multiple-event videos (e.g., ActivityNet Captions), and instruction-tuned video data. Evaluation metrics included IoU for grounding and captioning quality scores.
experiment.baseline: Baselines included existing Video LLMs (e.g., Video-LLaMA, Video-ChatGPT) and task-specific models. VTimeLLM significantly outperformed these on both tasks.
experiment.verification: The experiments directly validate the core idea that boundary-aware training improves temporal understanding, as VTimeLLM achieved higher accuracy in predicting event boundaries and generating temporally aligned captions.

entities.description: A time-sensitive multimodal large language model for long video understanding.

idea.background: Long video understanding remains challenging due to temporal dynamics and varying durations. Previous video large language models often lack precise temporal localization and suffer from fixed-length token sequences.
idea.novelty: Two key architectural innovations: a timestamp-aware frame encoder that integrates temporal information into visual features, and a sliding video Q-Former that adaptively generates variable-length video token sequences to handle videos of any length.
idea.contribution: Introduces TimeChat, which combines a timestamp-aware frame encoder and a sliding video Q-Former to enable effective long video understanding. Also constructs an instruction-tuning dataset with 125K instances across 6 tasks to improve instruction following.
idea.method: The timestamp-aware frame encoder takes each frame along with its timestamp to embed temporal context. The sliding video Q-Former processes the sequence of encoded frames with a sliding window mechanism outputting a variable number of tokens. The model is trained on an instruction-tuning dataset covering tasks like dense captioning, temporal grounding, and highlight detection.
idea.detail_reason: By binding visual content with timestamps, the model can associate events with specific times. The sliding video Q-Former allows the model to compress video information into a variable-length token sequence, avoiding information loss from fixed-length encoding and scaling gracefully with video duration.
idea.limitation: Not explicitly mentioned in the abstract. Potential limitations include reliance on the quality of timestamp annotations and possible difficulties with extremely long videos or complex temporal reasoning beyond the current tasks.

experiment.process: Evaluated on multiple video understanding tasks including dense captioning, temporal grounding, and highlight detection in zero-shot settings. Comparisons made with state-of-the-art video large language models.
experiment.technical_details: Used YouCook2, QVHighlights, and Charades-STA datasets. Metrics include F1, CIDEr, HIT@1, and R@1 with IoU thresholds.
experiment.baseline: State-of-the-art video large language models (not specified in abstract).
experiment.verification: Performance improvements on all tasks (e.g., +9.2 F1, +2.8 CIDEr on YouCook2; +5.8 HIT@1 on QVHighlights; +27.5 R@1 on Charades-STA) demonstrate the effectiveness of the temporal encoding and variable-length token sequence.

486c2df78cbb770a90a55f7fa3fe19102fba2c24.title: LLaMA-VID: An Image is Worth 2 Tokens in Large Language Models

entities.name: LLaMA-VID
entities.description: Novel Vision Language Model that represents each image/frame with two tokens (context and content) to reduce token count for video and image understanding.

entities.name: context token
entities.description: Token encoding overall image context based on user input.

entities.name: content token
entities.description: Token encapsulating visual cues in each frame.

idea.background: Vision Language Models (VLMs) excel at image captioning and visual question answering but face computational burdens when processing long videos due to excessive visual tokens. Existing methods struggle to efficiently handle hour-long videos while preserving critical information.
idea.novelty: LLaMA-VID introduces a dual-token strategy, representing each frame with a context token (user-input-driven) and a content token (visual cues), drastically reducing token overload without losing essential information. This enables support for hour-long videos and pushes the upper limit of VLMs with an extra context token.
idea.contribution: The primary method is representing each frame with two distinct tokens: a context token that encodes overall image context based on user input, and a content token that captures visual details. This compression allows existing frameworks to process long videos efficiently while maintaining performance.
idea.method: LLaMA-VID processes each frame through a vision encoder to extract features. A context token is generated by conditioning on user input (e.g., a question) and the global frame features, while a content token is derived from the local visual features. These two tokens replace the typical hundreds of visual tokens per frame. The token sequence is then fed into a large language model for tasks like video captioning or QA. The model is trained with standard cross-entropy loss on video-text datasets.
idea.detail_reason: The dual-token approach is effective because it decouples high-level context from detailed visual content, allowing the model to focus on task-relevant information. By compressing each frame into two tokens, the computational cost is dramatically reduced while retaining both semantic and fine-grained information, as validated by strong benchmark results.
idea.limitation: The method relies on user input to generate the context token, which may not be available or might be insufficient for open-ended video understanding. Additionally, compressing a frame into just two tokens could lose subtle visual details needed for fine-grained tasks. The assumption that two tokens are sufficient for all frames may not hold for highly complex scenes.

experiment.process: The model was evaluated on multiple video- and image-based benchmarks, comparing performance against existing VLMs.
experiment.technical_details: Implemented using a vision encoder (e.g., CLIP) and a large language model (e.g., LLaMA). Training details not specified, but code is available.
experiment.baseline: Compared against prior VLMs designed for video understanding, likely including VideoChat, LLaVA, and other token reduction methods.
experiment.verification: Experiments show that LLaMA-VID surpasses previous methods on most benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness of the dual-token compression for maintaining performance while reducing tokens.

references: LLaVA: Large Language and Vision Assistant
VideoChat: Chat-Centric Video Understanding
Perceiver: General Perception with Iterative Attention

e65b346d442e9962a4276dc1c1af2956d9d5f1eb.title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Models with Self-Generated Instructions

entities.name: Self-Instruct
entities.description: A framework for aligning language models by bootstrapping off their own generated instructions, inputs, and outputs.

entities.name: GPT3
entities.description: The vanilla pretrained language model used as the base for Self-Instruct tuning.

entities.name: Super-NaturalInstructions
entities.description: A benchmark dataset used to evaluate instruction-following performance.

entities.name: InstructGPT-001
entities.description: A model trained with private user data and human annotations, used as a baseline for comparison.

idea.background: Instruction-tuned language models generalize zero-shot to new tasks but rely heavily on human-written instruction data, which is often limited in quantity, diversity, and creativity. Prior work like InstructGPT uses human annotations to improve instruction following, but such data is expensive to collect.
idea.novelty: Self-Instruct introduces a bootstrap approach where a pretrained language model generates its own instruction data (instructions, inputs, and outputs), filters them for quality, and then fine-tunes itself on this self-generated data. This eliminates the need for human-written instructions and enables scalable creation of diverse training data.
idea.contribution: The primary method is a pipeline that generates instruction samples from a language model, filters out invalid or similar ones, and uses the remaining data to fine-tune the original model. Key components include instruction generation, input-output generation, and filtering based on similarity and validity. This framework significantly improves instruction-following without human annotations.
idea.method: The Self-Instruct pipeline starts with a small set of seed instructions. For each seed, the model generates new instructions, then for each new instruction it generates input-output pairs. Generated data is filtered to remove duplicates, invalid samples (e.g., input-output mismatches), and low-quality ones. The final dataset is used to fine-tune the original GPT3 model via standard supervised learning. The base model is GPT3, and fine-tuning uses the self-generated instruction data.
idea.detail_reason: Self-generated data is inherently diverse and tailored to the model's capabilities, reducing distribution mismatch between training and inference. The filtering step ensures high quality, and the bootstrapping approach allows iterative improvement without external data.
idea.limitation: The quality of generated instructions depends on the initial seed set and the base model's capabilities. The method may produce repetitive or low-quality instructions if the base model is weak. Additionally, it assumes the model can generate valid inputs and outputs for its own instructions, which may fail for tasks requiring external knowledge.

experiment.process: Self-Instruct was applied to vanilla GPT3 and evaluated on Super-NaturalInstructions. Human evaluation was also conducted on a curated set of expert-written novel tasks.
experiment.technical_details: GPT3 (175B parameters) was used as the base model. The pipeline generated 52k instruction samples. Filtering used ROUGE-L similarity to remove near-duplicates and heuristic checks for validity. Fine-tuning used cross-entropy loss on the generated (instruction, input, output) triplets.
experiment.baseline: Compared against original GPT3 (no tuning) and InstructGPT-001 (trained with human feedback). Also compared with tuning on existing public instruction datasets.
experiment.verification: The 33% absolute improvement over original GPT3 and near-parity with InstructGPT-001 on Super-NaturalInstructions demonstrates the effectiveness of self-generated data. Human evaluation showed a large margin over public instruction datasets, closing 5% absolute gap to expert-written instructions.

references: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback
Super-NaturalInstructions: Generalization via declarative instructions on 1600+ tasks
Language models are few-shot learners

0c0300f53c01ae609c97395c98de4c9d85d92876.title: MultiInstruct: Improving Multi-Modal Zero-Shot Learning via Instruction Tuning

entities.name: MultiInstruct
entities.description: First multimodal instruction tuning benchmark with 62 diverse tasks in unified seq-to-seq format.

entities.name: OFA
entities.description: Base pre-trained model for multimodal instruction tuning.

entities.name: Natural Instructions
entities.description: Large-scale text-only instruction dataset used for transfer learning.

entities.name: Sensitivity
entities.description: New evaluation metric to measure model sensitivity to instruction variety.

idea.background: Instruction tuning has shown promising zero-shot performance on NLP tasks, but has not been explored for vision and multimodal tasks. This work introduces the first multimodal instruction tuning benchmark to address this gap.
idea.novelty: The main innovation is the creation of MultiInstruct, the first multimodal instruction tuning benchmark covering 62 diverse tasks across 10 categories, each with 5 expert-written instructions. Additionally, a new evaluation metric, Sensitivity, is proposed to assess model robustness to instruction variations.
idea.contribution: The primary contributions are the MultiInstruct benchmark dataset and the demonstration that instruction tuning on multimodal tasks achieves strong zero-shot performance. The paper also shows the benefit of transfer learning from text-only instruction datasets (Natural Instructions) to improve performance.
idea.method: The approach uses OFA as the base pre-trained model, fine-tuned on multimodal tasks in a unified sequence-to-sequence format. Multiple transfer learning strategies are explored to leverage the Natural Instructions dataset. The model is evaluated on unseen multimodal tasks using zero-shot settings, and the Sensitivity metric quantifies performance consistency across different instruction phrasings.
idea.detail_reason: Transfer learning from a large-scale text-only instruction dataset (Natural Instructions) helps the model generalize better to unseen multimodal tasks because it exposes the model to diverse instruction formats. The unified seq-to-seq format simplifies task adaptation without task-specific architectures.
idea.limitation: The benchmark only uses tasks derived from 21 existing datasets, which may not cover all real-world scenarios. The experiments are limited to a single base model (OFA), and the sensitivity metric may not capture all aspects of instruction robustness.

experiment.process: The experiments involve fine-tuning OFA on MultiInstruct tasks and evaluating zero-shot performance on unseen multimodal tasks. Transfer learning from Natural Instructions is tested using multiple strategies. The Sensitivity metric is computed on held-out instructions.
experiment.technical_details: All tasks are formatted as sequence-to-sequence using a unified template. Transfer learning strategies include sequential fine-tuning and multi-task learning. The Sensitivity metric measures performance variance across 5 different instruction phrasings per task.
experiment.baseline: The baseline is OFA without instruction tuning and OFA fine-tuned only on the multimodal tasks without transfer from Natural Instructions.
experiment.verification: Strong zero-shot results on unseen tasks compared to baselines validate that instruction tuning and transfer learning improve generalization. The Sensitivity metric provides a new perspective on instruction robustness.

references: Instruction Tuning for Pre-trained Language Models
OFA: Unifying Architectures, Tasks, and Modalities Through a Simple Sequence-to-Sequence Learning Framework
Natural Instructions: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Instruction Tuning

9c9c5596f8a7a41fc31e5376a4ce1de9b93671ae.title: A Closer Look at Debiased Temporal Sentence Grounding in Videos: Dataset, Metric, and Approach

entities.name: Temporal Sentence Grounding in Videos (TSGV)
entities.description: Task of grounding natural language sentences in untrimmed videos.

entities.name: out-of-distribution (OOD) test
entities.description: Test setup where ground-truth moment distributions differ from training.

entities.name: dR@n,IoU=m
entities.description: New evaluation metric that discounts recall scores at small IoU thresholds.

idea.background: Temporal Sentence Grounding in Videos (TSGV) aims to ground language sentences in untrimmed videos. Recent studies have found that benchmark datasets have moment annotation biases, allowing simple baselines to achieve state-of-the-art performance without training.
idea.novelty: The paper proposes reorganizing datasets to create out-of-distribution (OOD) test splits and introduces a new evaluation metric 'dR@n,IoU=m' that discounts recall scores at small IoU thresholds to counter inflated evaluation from long ground-truth moments.
idea.contribution: The primary contributions are reorganized dataset splits with OOD test and a new metric dR@n,IoU=m. These provide more trustworthy benchmarking for TSGV.
idea.method: The authors re-organize two widely-used datasets to ensure ground-truth moment distributions differ between training and test splits. They define the dR@n,IoU=m metric which reduces the contribution of recall at low IoU thresholds. No new model architecture or training procedure is introduced.
idea.detail_reason: OOD test prevents models from exploiting biased moment distributions, and the discounted metric reduces the impact of long moments that inflate recall. This combination provides a more honest evaluation of model performance.
idea.limitation: The paper focuses only on evaluation protocols and does not propose new TSGV methods. The approach assumes that reorganized splits and the new metric are sufficient to debias evaluation; other biases may remain.

experiment.process: Experiments benchmark existing TSGV models on the reorganized datasets using the new evaluation metric.
experiment.technical_details: Reorganization of ActivityNet Captions and Charades-STA datasets; implementation of dR@n,IoU=m metric.
experiment.baseline: Several simple baselines and prior state-of-the-art models are compared.
experiment.verification: The new benchmarking results show improved monitoring of research progress, indicating the effectiveness of the proposed evaluation protocols.

references: Temporal Grounding in Videos: A Benchmark
Debiasing Temporal Sentence Grounding with Out-of-Distribution Detection
Rethinking the Evaluation of Temporal Sentence Grounding

1d26c947406173145a4665dd7ab255e03494ea28.title: GLM-130B: An Open Bilingual Pre-trained Model

entities.name: GLM-130B
entities.description: Bilingual (English and Chinese) pre-trained language model with 130 billion parameters, open-sourced.

entities.name: GPT-3 (davinci)
entities.description: Large language model by OpenAI with 175 billion parameters, used as baseline.

entities.name: OPT-175B
entities.description: Open pre-trained transformer model by Meta with 175 billion parameters.

entities.name: BLOOM-176B
entities.description: Multilingual large language model by BigScience with 176 billion parameters.

entities.name: ERNIE TITAN 3.0 260B
entities.description: Largest Chinese language model with 260 billion parameters by Baidu.

idea.background: Pre-training large language models (LLMs) at the 100B scale has been challenging, with few open-source models available. Previous models like GPT-3, OPT, and BLOOM have shown strong performance but often struggle with training stability and efficiency. This work aims to open-source a competitive 100B-scale bilingual model.
idea.novelty: GLM-130B is the first open-source 100B-scale model to achieve INT4 quantization without post-training and with minimal performance loss. It outperforms GPT-3 175B on English benchmarks while maintaining bilingual capability, a combination not seen in prior models like OPT or BLOOM.
idea.contribution: The paper details the training process including design choices for architecture, training strategies for stability and efficiency, and engineering solutions to handle loss spikes and divergence. It also presents a unique scaling property enabling effective INT4 quantization.
idea.method: GLM-130B uses a transformer-based architecture trained on bilingual data. Key design choices include specific initialization, learning rate scheduling, and gradient clipping to avoid divergence. The model leverages a scaling property that allows INT4 quantization without post-training, reducing inference cost to run on 4×RTX 3090 GPUs.
idea.detail_reason: The INT4 quantization exploits an observed scaling property of GLM-130B where performance degradation from quantization diminishes with model scale. Training stability is achieved through careful selection of hyperparameters and early detection of loss spikes, preventing catastrophic divergence.
idea.limitation: The paper does not explore fine-tuning performance or broader task coverage beyond benchmarks. Training faced unexpected loss spikes and divergence, indicating potential instability at scale. The model's bilingual capability may not match specialized monolingual models in certain domains.

experiment.process: GLM-130B was evaluated on a wide range of popular English and Chinese benchmarks, comparing against GPT-3 175B, OPT-175B, BLOOM-176B, and ERNIE TITAN 3.0 260B.
experiment.technical_details: INT4 quantization was applied directly to the pretrained model without any post-training, exploiting the scaling property. Inference was demonstrated on 4×RTX 3090 GPUs.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include GPT-3 (davinci), OPT-175B, BLOOM-176B, and ERNIE TITAN 3.0 260B.
experiment.verification: The experiments show GLM-130B significantly outperforms GPT-3 on English benchmarks and outperforms ERNIE TITAN on Chinese benchmarks, while maintaining performance within INT4 quantization.

references: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3)
OPT: Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models
BLOOM: A 176B-Parameter Open-Access Multilingual Language Model

d3135733aa39dec20ce72aa138589dda27c8406d.title: Learn to Explain: Multimodal Reasoning via Thought Chains for Science Question Answering

entities.name: ScienceQA
entities.description: A large-scale multimodal science question answering benchmark with ~21k multiple-choice questions, lectures, and explanations.

entities.name: Chain-of-Thought (CoT)
entities.description: A reasoning approach that generates intermediate steps (lectures and explanations) to improve answer accuracy.

entities.name: GPT-3
entities.description: A large language model used in few-shot setting with CoT prompting.

entities.name: UnifiedQA
entities.description: A unified question answering model fine-tuned with CoT for science QA.

idea.background: Science question answering benchmarks are used to evaluate multi-hop reasoning and interpretability of AI systems. However, existing datasets are limited to textual-only modality, small scale, and lack answer annotations or explanations.
idea.novelty: This paper introduces ScienceQA, the first large-scale multimodal science QA dataset with annotations of answers, lectures, and explanations. It employs chain-of-thought (CoT) to generate lectures and explanations, mimicking multi-hop reasoning, which is a novel application of CoT in the multimodal science domain.
idea.contribution: The primary methods involve fine-tuning UnifiedQA and using few-shot GPT-3 to generate chain-of-thought (lectures and explanations) for answering ScienceQA questions. The core components are the CoT generation and integration into QA models. This improves performance over baselines without CoT.
idea.method: The approach uses language models (GPT-3 and UnifiedQA) to generate a chain of thought consisting of a lecture and an explanation for each question. The CoT is then concatenated with the question to produce the answer. For GPT-3, few-shot prompting with CoT examples is used; for UnifiedQA, fine-tuning is performed on ScienceQA with CoT targets. The models are multimodal, accepting text and image inputs from ScienceQA.
idea.detail_reason: Generating lectures and explanations as chain-of-thought helps the model perform explicit multi-hop reasoning, breaking down complex science questions into intermediate steps. This structured reasoning improves answer accuracy because it leverages the model's ability to generate coherent and relevant knowledge, as evidenced by the performance gains (1.20% in GPT-3, 3.99% in UnifiedQA).
idea.limitation: The paper does not explicitly discuss limitations, but potential shortcomings include reliance on the quality of generated CoT (which may be hallucinated), limited domain diversity (only science topics), and the benchmark being multiple-choice only, which may not capture open-ended reasoning.

experiment.process: Experiments evaluate ScienceQA by comparing model accuracy with and without chain-of-thought. Few-shot GPT-3 and fine-tuned UnifiedQA are tested, and results are reported as accuracy improvements.
experiment.technical_details: GPT-3 is used with few-shot CoT prompting (8 examples). UnifiedQA is fine-tuned on ScienceQA with added CoT tokens. Both models handle multimodal inputs (text + images).
experiment.baseline: Baselines include the same models without chain-of-thought (i.e., directly predicting answer without generating lectures/explanations).
experiment.verification: The improvement in accuracy (1.20% for GPT-3, 3.99% for UnifiedQA) demonstrates that adding CoT effectively enhances reasoning.

references: Science Question Answering (ScienceQA) Benchmark
Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
UnifiedQA: Crossing Format Boundaries With a Single QA System

d766bffc357127e0dc86dd69561d5aeb520d6f4c.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback

entities.name: InstructGPT
entities.description: Models fine-tuned from GPT-3 using supervised learning on demonstrations and reinforcement learning from human feedback.

entities.name: GPT-3
entities.description: Large language model with 175B parameters; used as base for InstructGPT fine-tuning.

entities.name: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)
entities.description: Method to fine-tune models using rankings of outputs as reward signal.

entities.name: Supervised Fine-Tuning
entities.description: Initial training on labeler-written demonstrations to mimic desired behavior.

idea.background: Large language models scaled in size do not inherently improve alignment with user intent; they can produce untruthful, toxic, or unhelpful outputs. Prior work on alignment includes scaling model size, but this paper explores fine-tuning with human feedback to directly optimize for user preferences.
idea.novelty: This paper demonstrates that fine-tuning language models with human feedback (RLHF) significantly improves alignment with user intent, allowing a 1.3B parameter model (InstructGPT) to be preferred over a 175B GPT-3 on human evaluations. It also shows improvements in truthfulness and reductions in toxicity.
idea.contribution: The primary methods are supervised fine-tuning on labeler demonstrations followed by reinforcement learning from human feedback on rankings of model outputs. Core components include a dataset of labeler-written prompts and demonstrations, and a dataset of output rankings used to train a reward model for RL.
idea.method: Starting with labeler-written prompts and prompts from the OpenAI API, a dataset of labeler demonstrations of desired behavior is collected. GPT-3 is fine-tuned on this dataset via supervised learning. Then, a dataset of model output rankings is collected from labelers, and a reward model is trained on these rankings. The supervised model is further fine-tuned using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to maximize the reward, yielding InstructGPT.
idea.detail_reason: Human feedback directly captures nuanced preferences that are hard to specify via rules. Supervised learning provides a good initialization, and RLHF optimizes for the actual reward (human preference) rather than imitation, leading to better alignment.
idea.limitation: The reliance on human labelers introduces potential biases and scalability challenges. The paper does not fully address alignment with long-term goals or safe behavior beyond the prompt distribution tested. Performance on some public NLP benchmarks shows minimal regression but not improvement.

experiment.process: Labelers write prompts and demonstrate desired outputs for supervised fine-tuning. Then, labelers rank multiple model outputs for various prompts; these rankings train a reward model. The supervised model is further fine-tuned with PPO using this reward model. Human evaluators compare InstructGPT outputs to GPT-3 outputs on the prompt distribution.
experiment.technical_details: Uses GPT-3 model variants (1.3B, 6B, 175B). Supervised fine-tuning on ~13k demonstrations. Reward model trained on ~33k rankings. PPO training with KL penalty to avoid over-optimization.
experiment.baseline: GPT-3 (175B) without fine-tuning, as well as supervised fine-tuned models without RLHF.
experiment.verification: Human evaluators prefer InstructGPT outputs over GPT-3 outputs on 85% of prompts. Additional metrics show InstructGPT models are more truthful (e.g., on TruthfulQA) and less toxic, while maintaining performance on standard NLP benchmarks.

references: Learning to summarize from human feedback
Deep reinforcement learning from human preferences
Fine-Tuning Language Models from Human Preferences

26218bdcc3945c7edae7aa2adbfba4cd820a2df3.title: Flamingo: a Visual Language Model for Few-Shot Learning

entities.name: Flamingo
entities.description: A family of Visual Language Models for few-shot learning that bridges pretrained vision-only and language-only models.

entities.name: Visual Language Model (VLM)
entities.description: A model that processes both visual and textual data, enabling multimodal understanding.

entities.name: In-context few-shot learning
entities.description: The ability to rapidly adapt to novel tasks using only a handful of annotated examples provided in the input context.

idea.background: Building models that can rapidly adapt to novel tasks using few annotated examples is an open challenge in multimodal machine learning. Prior work often requires task-specific fine-tuning or large annotated datasets. Flamingo addresses this by leveraging pretrained vision-only and language-only models.
idea.novelty: Flamingo introduces key architectural innovations to bridge pretrained vision-only and language-only models, handle arbitrarily interleaved visual and textual data, and ingest both images and videos as inputs. This enables training on large-scale multimodal web corpora, endowing the model with in-context few-shot learning capabilities.
idea.contribution: The primary contribution is the Flamingo family of Visual Language Models that can be trained on interleaved text and images from the web, enabling few-shot adaptation to diverse image and video tasks including visual question-answering, captioning, and multiple-choice VQA. The model uses a single architecture for open-ended and close-ended tasks.
idea.method: Flamingo uses a transformer-based architecture that bridges frozen pretrained vision and language encoders with learnable components. It processes sequences of arbitrarily interleaved visual (images or videos) and textual data. The model is trained on a large-scale multimodal web corpus using a language modeling objective, which allows it to perform in-context learning without task-specific fine-tuning.
idea.detail_reason: Bridging pretrained models leverages their strong individual representations while requiring fewer parameters to train. Training on interleaved web data exposes the model to diverse multimodal contexts, crucial for in-context learning. The architectural flexibility to handle both images and videos in a unified manner broadens applicability.
idea.limitation: The abstract does not discuss limitations, but potential shortcomings include reliance on the quality and diversity of web data, possible biases in pretrained components, and computational cost of large-scale training. The model's performance may also degrade on tasks requiring specialized visual reasoning not well represented in the training data.

experiment.process: Flamingo is evaluated on a variety of image and video tasks: open-ended visual question-answering, captioning, and multiple-choice visual question-answering. The model is tested in a few-shot setting where a small number of annotated examples are provided as context.
experiment.technical_details: The model is a transformer with cross-attention between vision and language modalities. Training uses a next-token prediction objective on a large web corpus. Evaluation uses standard benchmarks for each task, with the model prompted with interleaved examples and queries.
experiment.baseline: Baselines likely include prior few-shot multimodal models or fine-tuned versions of pretrained models, though specific baselines are not mentioned in the abstract.
experiment.verification: The experiments demonstrate that a single Flamingo model can rapidly adapt to different tasks and input modalities, supporting the claim of few-shot learning capabilities through in-context learning.

references: CLIP: Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision
ALIGN: Scaling Up Visual and Vision-Language Representation Learning With Noisy Text Supervision
Few-Shot Learning via Language Model Prompting

a970c8fadef8497576660b288c52c0ec8eebdc12.title: Zero-Shot Video Question Answering via Frozen Bidirectional Language Models

entities.name: FrozenBiLM
entities.description: Proposed method for zero-shot VideoQA using frozen bidirectional language models.

entities.name: Bidirectional language model (BiLM)
entities.description: Language model that uses context from both left and right, pretrained on text-only data.

entities.name: Masked language modeling
entities.description: Inference technique where the answer token is masked and predicted by the model.

idea.background: Video question answering (VideoQA) requires diverse multi-modal data for training, but manual annotation is tedious and limits scalability. Recent zero-shot methods adapt frozen autoregressive language models pretrained on text-only data to multi-modal inputs, but they have limitations.
idea.novelty: This work uses frozen bidirectional language models (BiLM) instead of autoregressive ones, providing a stronger and cheaper alternative for zero-shot VideoQA. The approach leverages bidirectional context for better answer prediction.
idea.contribution: The primary methods include combining visual inputs with a frozen BiLM using light trainable modules, training these modules on Web-scraped multi-modal data, and performing zero-shot VideoQA via masked language modeling where the masked text is the answer.
idea.method: The architecture consists of a frozen bidirectional language model (BiLM) with lightweight trainable modules to fuse visual features. The training procedure uses Web-scraped multi-modal data (e.g., video-text pairs) to train the visual modules while keeping the BiLM frozen. During inference, the question and video are input, and the answer is obtained by masking a token in the question or a placeholder, then using the BiLM to predict the masked token. Key design choices include using a BiLM for bidirectional context and light modules to preserve the frozen nature.
idea.detail_reason: Bidirectional language models capture context from both directions, which is beneficial for question answering where the answer depends on surrounding text. Using light trainable modules avoids fine-tuning the entire language model, making it computationally efficient and preserving pretrained knowledge.
idea.limitation: The approach assumes the answer is a single token that can be masked, limiting complex answers. It also relies on Web-scraped data for training the visual modules, which may introduce noise and biases. The method is evaluated on multiple datasets but may not generalize to all video domains.

experiment.process: The proposed FrozenBiLM is trained on Web-scraped multi-modal data (e.g., instructional videos with captions) and evaluated zero-shot on multiple VideoQA datasets without any manual QA annotation for those datasets.
experiment.technical_details: Uses a frozen bidirectional language model (e.g., BERT) as the text backbone. Visual features are extracted from video frames (e.g., using a pretrained video encoder) and projected into the language model's embedding space via light modules (e.g., linear layers or small transformers). Training is done with a masked language modeling objective on the Web-scraped data.
experiment.baseline: Compared against prior zero-shot VideoQA methods, especially those using frozen autoregressive language models (e.g., Frozen), as well as fully supervised methods on some datasets.
experiment.verification: Experiments on eight datasets (LSMDC-FiB, iVQA, MSRVTT-QA, MSVD-QA, ActivityNet-QA, TGIF-FrameQA, How2QA, TVQA) show significant improvements over state-of-the-art zero-shot methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of the bidirectional approach.

references: Frozen in Time: A Joint Video and Image Encoder for End-to-End Retrieval
VideoBERT: A Joint Model for Video and Language Representation Learning
Learning to Answer Questions from Videos

78281482c1fdad8e167bab39cc9955c73d58ae8f.title: EVA: Exploring the Limits of Masked Visual Representation Learning at Scale

entities.name: EVA
entities.description: Vision-centric foundation model based on vanilla ViT, pre-trained to reconstruct masked image-text aligned vision features.

entities.name: Masked Visual Representation Learning
entities.description: Pre-training approach that reconstructs masked patches using visible context, applied here to vision features aligned with text.

entities.name: Image-Text Aligned Vision Features
entities.description: Target representations for reconstruction, derived from multimodal alignment (e.g., CLIP), encoding high-level semantic information.

idea.background: Masked image modeling (e.g., MAE) and contrastive language-image pre-training (e.g., CLIP) have advanced visual representations. However, scaling vision transformers to billions of parameters with only public data remains challenging. EVA addresses this by combining masked reconstruction with image-text aligned targets.
idea.novelty: EVA introduces a pretext task that reconstructs masked image-text aligned vision features (not raw pixels), enabling scaling to 1B parameters with public data. It observes qualitative changes in transfer learning at scale, e.g., matching performance on LVIS and COCO instance segmentation.
idea.contribution: EVA uses a vanilla ViT encoder pre-trained via masked reconstruction of CLIP-like visual features. The core innovation is the choice of reconstruction target: high-level, semantically rich features from image-text alignment. This allows efficient scaling and strong transfer to diverse tasks.
idea.method: Architecture is a vanilla ViT. Pre-training masks random patches of input images and learns to reconstruct the corresponding image-text aligned vision features (e.g., from a frozen CLIP vision encoder) using the visible patches. Training uses only publicly accessible data (e.g., ImageNet, CC3M/12M). After pre-training, EVA is fine-tuned on downstream tasks.
idea.detail_reason: Reconstructing image-text aligned forces the model to capture high-level semantic concepts rather than low-level textures, leading to better transfer. Scaling reveals emergent properties in instance segmentation, likely due to more discriminative features for fine-grained categories.
idea.limitation: EVA relies on pre-existing image-text aligned features from a teacher model (e.g., CLIP), which may introduce bias. The method assumes public data suffices for large-scale pre-training, but performance on highly specialized domains is unexplored. Additionally, the computational cost of pre-training a 1B parameter model is significant.

experiment.process: EVA is pre-trained on large public image datasets (e.g., ImageNet-21K, CC3M+12M). Then fine-tuned on various downstream tasks: image classification (ImageNet), video action recognition (Kinetics), object detection (COCO), instance segmentation (COCO, LVIS), semantic segmentation (ADE20K).
experiment.technical_details: Uses vanilla ViT with 1B parameters. Pre-training: mask ratio 75%, target features from a pre-trained CLIP ViT-L/14. Optimization: AdamW, cosine schedule. Fine-tuning adapts heads per task.
experiment.baseline: Compared against MAE, CLIP, MoCo v3, and other ViT variants. Also compared against prior state-of-the-art on each benchmark.
experiment.verification: EVA achieves new SOTA on all tasks. The qualitative change is highlighted by nearly equal performance on LVIS (1236 categories) and COCO (80 categories), whereas prior models struggle on LVIS.

references: Masked Autoencoders Are Scalable Vision Learners (MAE)
Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision (CLIP)
An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale (ViT)
